section.title: Judgment and a Promise of Restoration
section.sectionRationale: The vision that began with idolatry in the temple now turns to the wicked counselors who plot violence in Jerusalem, and to the exiles the city despises as cast off. In the middle of this scene of judgment, right where hope seems impossible, God speaks His clearest promise yet: a new heart, a new spirit, and a gathering home, before the glory finally lifts off the city altogether.
section.thesis: God judges Jerusalem's wicked leaders and their false confidence that the exiles are 'far from the LORD', yet in the same breath He promises the very exiles they despise a new heart and a new spirit, proving that His discipline always serves His larger purpose of restoration, a purpose brought to completion in the new covenant secured by Christ.
section.frameNote: Three movements: judgment on the wicked counselors who treat Jerusalem as a cooking pot and its people as meat, ending in Pelatiah's sudden death; the promise to the scattered exiles of gathering, a new heart, and a new spirit; and the glory's final rise from the city to the mountain on its east side.

verses.text: Then the Spirit lifted me up and brought me to the gate of the house of the LORD that faces east. And there at the entrance of the gate were twenty-five men. Among them I saw Jaazaniah son of Azzur and Pelatiah son of Benaiah, who were leaders of the people.
And the LORD said to me, “Son of man, these are the men who plot evil and give wicked counsel in this city.
They are saying, ‘Is not the time near to build houses? The city is the cooking pot, and we are the meat.’
Therefore prophesy against them; prophesy, O son of man!”
And the Spirit of the LORD fell upon me and told me to declare that this is what the LORD says: “That is what you are thinking, O house of Israel; and I know the thoughts that arise in your minds.
You have multiplied those you killed in this city and filled its streets with the dead.
Therefore this is what the Lord GOD says: The slain you have laid within this city are the meat, and the city is the pot; but I will remove you from it.
You fear the sword, so I will bring the sword against you, declares the Lord GOD.
I will bring you out of the city and deliver you into the hands of foreigners, and I will execute judgments against you.
You will fall by the sword, and I will judge you even to the borders of Israel. Then you will know that I am the LORD.
The city will not be a pot for you, nor will you be the meat within it. I will judge you even to the borders of Israel.
Then you will know that I am the LORD. For you have neither followed My statutes nor practiced My ordinances, but you have conformed to the ordinances of the nations around you.”
Now as I was prophesying, Pelatiah son of Benaiah died. Then I fell facedown and cried out in a loud voice, “Oh, Lord GOD, will You bring the remnant of Israel to a complete end?”

units.label: A new heart and a new spirit
units.note: gathering, cleansing, and transformation promised to the exiles

verses.text: Then the word of the LORD came to me, saying,

“Son of man, your brothers, your relatives, your fellow exiles, and the whole house of Israel, are those of whom the people of Jerusalem have said, ‘They are far away from the LORD; this land has been given to us as a possession.’

Therefore declare that this is what the Lord GOD says: ‘Although I sent them far away among the nations and scattered them among the countries, yet for a little while I have been a {{miqdash_meat|sanctuary}} for them in the countries to which they have gone.’

Therefore declare that this is what the Lord GOD says: ‘I will gather you from the peoples and assemble you from the countries to which you have been scattered, and I will give back to you the land of Israel.’
When they return to it, they will remove all its detestable things and all its abominations.
And I will give them {{lev_echad|singleness of heart}} and put a {{ruach_chadashah|new spirit}} within them; I will remove their heart of stone and give them a heart of flesh,
so that they may follow My statutes, keep My ordinances, and practice them. Then they will be My people, and I will be their God.
But as for those whose hearts pursue detestable things and abominations, I will bring their conduct down upon their own heads, declares the Lord GOD.”

units.label: The glory rises from the city
units.note: to the mountain on the east

verses.text: Then the cherubim, with the wheels beside them, spread their wings, and the glory of the God of Israel was above them.
And the glory of the LORD rose up from within the city and stood over the mountain east of the city.
And the Spirit lifted me up and carried me back to Chaldea, to the exiles in the vision given by the Spirit of God. After the vision had gone up from me,
I told the exiles everything the LORD had shown me.

miqdash_meat.gloss: A sanctuary in small measure, or a little sanctuary. Even in exile, far from the ruined temple, God graciously makes Himself present with His scattered people in a lesser but real way, a foretaste of His presence not being confined to one building or land.

lev_echad.gloss: One heart, or singleness of heart. Where the people's hearts were divided between the LORD and idols, God promises to unify their affection and loyalty entirely toward Himself.

ruach_chadashah.gloss: A new spirit. Paired with the removal of a heart of stone and the gift of a heart of flesh, this promise anticipates the New Testament gift of the Holy Spirit who enables obedience from within rather than mere external conformity.

basic.context: Ezekiel is shown the very leaders who plot violence in Jerusalem and dismiss the exiles as cut off from the LORD. God judges those leaders, yet turns immediately to comfort the exiles with an astonishing promise: a new heart and a new spirit, so that they will finally, truly, obey Him. Only after this promise does the glory finally lift off the city and move east.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real vision given to Ezekiel among the exiles, addressing an actual dispute over who truly belonged to God, those left in Jerusalem or those already deported. The hardest, most needed lesson is that outward proximity to the temple, or geography, or ancestry, does not guarantee closeness to God; genuine transformation of heart does.
basic.originalLanguages: God calls Himself a miqdash me'at, a little sanctuary, to the scattered exiles, showing His presence is not chained to one building. He then promises lev echad, one heart, and ruach chadashah, a new spirit, replacing a heart of stone with a heart of flesh, the clearest Old Testament anticipation of the inward transformation the New Testament calls the new birth.
basic.typology: The promised new heart and new spirit anticipate the new covenant of Jeremiah 31:31-34 and its fulfillment in the gift of the Holy Spirit, who now writes God's law on believers' hearts, 2 Corinthians 3:3, Hebrews 8:10, enabling from within the obedience the old covenant could only command from outside.
basic.christ: Jesus is the mediator of the new covenant Ezekiel here anticipates, Luke 22:20, Hebrews 8:6-13; through His Spirit, poured out at Pentecost, He accomplishes exactly what Ezekiel 11:19-20 promises, replacing hearts of stone with hearts responsive to God, so that His people can at last keep His statutes, not by their own effort but by His indwelling Spirit.
basic.god: God is just, holding wicked leaders accountable for the violence and false confidence that fills the city; yet He is also tenderly gracious to the very people the city despises, promising the exiles a sanctuary in exile, a homecoming, and a transformed heart, proving His judgment and His mercy flow from the same covenant faithfulness.
basic.crossReferences: Jeremiah 31:31-34 promises the new covenant written on the heart. 2 Corinthians 3:3 and Hebrews 8:10 apply this promise directly to believers in Christ. Ezekiel 36:26-27 repeats and expands this same new heart and new spirit promise. Acts 2 records the Spirit's outpouring that begins its fulfillment.
basic.oneStory: This is the moment judgment and hope meet in the same breath: God judges the proud counselors of a doomed city, yet promises the despised exiles a heart transplant that no circumstance can undo. Every page after this runs toward the day that promise is kept fully in Christ, who gives His Spirit so that God's people at last love and obey Him from the heart.

context.literary: The chapter moves from a specific indictment of named leaders, using the vivid image of the pot and the meat, into a direct promise to the exiles, before ending with the glory's final departure from the city, closing the vision begun in chapter 8.
context.historical: Written to exiles who were mocked by those still in Jerusalem as having lost their standing before God, 'They are far away from the LORD; this land has been given to us as a possession,' this passage reverses that judgment entirely, showing the exiles, not the complacent remnant in the city, are the ones God will restore.
context.canonical: Ezekiel 11:19-20 is one of the Old Testament's clearest statements of the new covenant hope later expanded in Ezekiel 36 and Jeremiah 31, and directly quoted or echoed by the writer of Hebrews when describing the covenant Christ mediates.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To correct Jerusalem's false confidence that its inhabitants alone belonged to God, to pronounce judgment on the city's violent leadership, and to comfort the exiles with the promise that God Himself will gather them, cleanse them, and give them new hearts.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific historical judgment and a specific promise to a specific exiled community; it is not prescribing a ritual. Its enduring, prescriptive truth is that true belonging to God is a matter of the heart, not geography or status, and that God alone can accomplish the transformation His law requires.

debates.question: Is the promise of a new heart in verse 19 fulfilled at the return from exile, or does it await a later, fuller fulfillment?

views.label: Partially fulfilled at the return, fully fulfilled in Christ
views.case: Many note that the post-exilic community, though restored to the land, still struggled with the same heart problems Ezekiel describes, showing the return itself was not the promise's full accomplishment; the New Testament treats the new heart and Spirit as fulfilled in believers through Christ, Hebrews 8:8-12.

views.label: A single, unified promise realized progressively
views.case: Others read the promise as one continuous divine work, begun in a limited way at the return from Babylon and brought to fullness at Pentecost, so that both moments are genuine, if unequal, installments of the same promise.

debates.bearing: Both views agree the promise finds its true depth and permanence only in the new covenant secured by Christ and applied by His Spirit, not in mere geographic return.

study.originalLanguages: God calls Himself a miqdash me'at, a little sanctuary, to the scattered exiles, proof His presence is never chained to one building or land. He then promises lev echad, one heart, and ruach chadashah, a new spirit, replacing a heart of stone with a heart of flesh, the clearest Old Testament anticipation of the New Testament's inward transformation by the Spirit.

typology.type: The new heart and new spirit promised to the exiles, Ezekiel 11:19-20
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the new covenant Christ mediates and the Spirit He pours out, who writes God's law on the heart, 2 Corinthians 3:3, Hebrews 8:10.

typology.type: The sanctuary in small measure among the scattered exiles, Ezekiel 11:16
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates God's presence no longer confined to a single temple but dwelling among His gathered people everywhere, fulfilled in the church as the temple of the Spirit, 1 Corinthians 3:16.

study.christ: Jesus is the mediator of the new covenant this chapter anticipates, taking the cup and saying, 'This cup is the new covenant in My blood,' Luke 22:20. Hebrews 8:6-13 explicitly ties Christ's priestly work to the fulfillment of exactly this kind of promise, a law written on hearts rather than tablets. Through His Spirit, poured out at Pentecost, He accomplishes what Ezekiel 11:19-20 promises: hearts of stone replaced with hearts able, at last, to keep God's statutes.
study.god: God is just, holding accountable the wicked counselors who fill Jerusalem with violence and false confidence; yet He is tenderly gracious to the exiles the city despises, promising them a sanctuary in exile, a homecoming, and transformed hearts, showing that His judgment and His mercy both flow from the same covenant faithfulness.

crossReferences.note: Promises the new covenant written on the heart, the same hope Ezekiel voices here.
Repeats and expands the promise of a new heart and new spirit given by God's own Spirit.
Paul applies this heart-transformation language directly to believers in Christ.
Explicitly connects Christ's mediatorial work to the fulfillment of this new covenant promise.

study.oneStory: This is the moment judgment and hope meet in the same breath: God judges the proud counselors of a doomed city, yet promises the despised exiles a heart transplant no circumstance can undo. Every page after this runs toward the day that promise is kept fully in Christ, who gives His Spirit so that God's people at last love and obey Him from the heart.

sermon.title: Does God Ever Give Up on People? (Ezek. 10-11)
sermon.why: Carson traces both the judgment on Jerusalem's wicked counselors and the astonishing promise of a new heart and new spirit within chapter 11, showing how the exile's darkest moment carries within it the seed of God's greatest covenant hope, exactly the tension this study is built around.

quiz.q: What false confidence do the twenty-five leaders express with the image of the pot and the meat?

options.text: That Jerusalem's walls make its inhabitants safe like meat protected in a cooking pot, while plotting violence and killing.
options.note: Verses 3, 6-7 show this image expressed pride in the city's supposed safety even while filling it with the slain.

options.text: That Jerusalem's food supply would never run low.
options.note: The imagery is about false security, not literal food supply.

options.text: That the exiles would soon return and rebuild the city together.
options.note: The leaders are dismissive of the exiles, not planning a reunion with them.

options.text: That Ezekiel himself was the source of the city's danger.
options.note: The danger comes from the leaders' own wicked counsel and violence, not from Ezekiel.

quiz.q: What does God promise to be for the exiles while they are scattered among the nations?

options.text: A sanctuary in small measure, His presence remaining with them even away from the ruined temple.
options.note: Verse 16 makes this explicit promise of God's nearness in exile.

options.text: Complete silence and absence until they physically return to the land.
options.note: The text promises the opposite: God's presence with them during the exile itself.

options.text: A replacement temple built by their own hands in Babylon.
options.note: No physical temple is built; the sanctuary described is God's own presence, not a structure.

options.text: A temporary curse with no promise of restoration.
options.note: The passage promises far more than mere endurance; it promises gathering and transformation.

quiz.q: What does God promise to give His people in verse 19?

options.text: Singleness of heart and a new spirit, removing a heart of stone and giving a heart of flesh.
options.note: Verse 19 states this transformation explicitly and directly.

options.text: A new set of written laws to replace the old ones.
options.note: The promise is an inward transformation of the heart, not a replacement legal code.

options.text: Wealth and prosperity as a reward for enduring exile.
options.note: The promise concerns inward transformation, not material reward.

options.text: A new set of priests to replace the corrupt ones in Jerusalem.
options.note: The passage addresses the people's hearts broadly, not specifically a reformed priesthood.

quiz.q: What happens to Pelatiah while Ezekiel is prophesying?

options.text: He dies suddenly, prompting Ezekiel to cry out in fear for the remnant of Israel.
options.note: Verse 13 records his death and Ezekiel's anguished cry in response.

options.text: He repents publicly and leads the other counselors to repentance.
options.note: The text records his death, not a scene of repentance.

options.text: He is taken into exile alongside Ezekiel's audience.
options.note: He dies in Jerusalem; he is not exiled.

options.text: He is promoted to a position of greater authority.
options.note: His death, not promotion, is what the text records.

quiz.q: Where does the glory of the LORD finally come to rest at the end of this chapter?

options.text: Above the mountain east of the city.
options.note: Verse 23 states the glory rose from within the city and stood over the mountain east of it.

options.text: It returns permanently to the Most Holy Place.
options.note: The chapter records continued departure, not a return to the temple's inner room.

options.text: It disappears entirely with no further mention in the book.
options.note: Ezekiel 43 later promises and records the glory's future return.

options.text: It remains at the east gate of the temple itself.
options.note: By this chapter's end the glory has moved further, to the mountain east of the city.

questions: The Jerusalem leaders felt falsely secure, treating the city like a pot that could protect them regardless of their violence and injustice. Where might you be trusting in circumstances or status rather than a right relationship with God?

Those in exile were despised by those still in the city, yet it was the exiles God promised to restore. Who do you tend to write off as 'far from the LORD', and how does this passage challenge that judgment?

God promises a new heart and new spirit that enables obedience from within rather than mere outward compliance. Where in your life are you relying on willpower rather than the Spirit's transforming work?
Ezekiel cried out in anguish when he saw judgment fall, even on a wicked man. How do you hold together grief over sin's consequences with confidence in God's ultimate promise of restoration?

God calls Himself a 'sanctuary in small measure' to His exiled people, present with them even far from the temple. Where do you need to remember that God's presence is not confined to a building, a routine, or a season of ease?

translationNotes.note: The proverb 'the city is the cooking pot, and we are the meat' likely expressed a boast of protected privilege; God ironically reverses its meaning in verse 7, applying it instead to the slain the leaders themselves have filled the city with.

The BSB smooths an appositional phrase describing the exiles as 'your brothers, your relatives, your fellow exiles, and the whole house of Israel', emphasizing the totality of those Jerusalem's residents had written off.

Some manuscripts and versions read 'one heart' rather than 'a new heart', both conveying the same basic promise of undivided, transformed devotion; the BSB's 'singleness of heart' captures the sense of a heart no longer split between the LORD and idols.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit. Sermon link verified via live web search and fetch this session, The Gospel Coalition's Carson Center podcast, the same episode covering Ezekiel 10-11 as the previous study.
